initially.example: Initially, the baby could only cry to communicate.

communicating.definition: Sharing information, ideas, or feelings with another person.

gestures.definition: Movements of the hands, face, or body that express meaning.
gestures.example: A wave is a common gesture for saying hello.

coercive.definition: Using force or pressure to make someone do something.
coercive.example: Coercive methods do not build trust.

initiate.example: The child tried to initiate a game.

forerunner.definition: Something that comes before and leads to something else.
forerunner.example: A smile is a forerunner to laughter.

conversation.definition: A talk between two or more people.
conversation.example: We had a long conversation about school.

reciprocity.definition: A situation where two people give and receive equally.
reciprocity.example: A good friendship is based on reciprocity.

shush.definition: To tell someone to be quiet.
shush.example: Please don't shush the baby when she cries.

pacifier.definition: A rubber object that babies suck on to feel calm.
pacifier.example: The baby stopped crying when given a pacifier.

conjunction.example: The medicine works in conjunction with rest.

soothe.definition: To make someone feel calm and less upset.
soothe.example: Soft music can soothe a crying baby.

stopper.definition: Something used to block or close an opening; here, something that stops communication.
stopper.example: Don't use the pacifier as a stopper for your child's voice.

gag.definition: To stop someone from speaking or expressing themselves.
gag.example: We should never gag a child's natural expression.

articulate.definition: To express thoughts or feelings clearly in words.
articulate.example: Young children cannot articulate their emotions well.

cues.definition: Signs or signals that show what someone needs or feels.
cues.example: Parents learn to read their baby's cues.

observing.example: By observing the child, you can understand their needs.

viewpoint.definition: A way of thinking about something; a perspective.
viewpoint.example: Everyone has their own viewpoint on life.

expanding.example: Reading helps in expanding your knowledge.

respected.example: Every child deserves to be respected.

innately.definition: In a way that is natural and present from birth.
innately.example: Babies innately seek closeness with their parents.

granted.example: Don't take your health for granted.

gaze.definition: To look at something steadily for a long time.
gaze.example: She would gaze at her sleeping baby for hours.

expressions.definition: The looks on a person's face that show feelings.
expressions.example: Her expressions showed she was happy.

gradually.definition: Slowly, over a period of time.
gradually.example: The baby gradually learned to walk.

observe.example: Take time to observe your child at play.

impact.definition: A strong effect or influence on something.
impact.example: Parents have a big impact on their children.

indeed.definition: Used to emphasize something is true.
indeed.example: It was indeed a wonderful day.

deliberate.example: Her kind words were a deliberate choice.

intentions.definition: Plans or aims to do something.
intentions.example: His intentions were good, even if the result was not.

recipient.example: The child was the recipient of a gift.

sponge.definition: Something that absorbs; here, a person who only takes in without giving back.
sponge.example: A student should not be just a sponge in class.

fulfilling.definition: Making you feel happy and satisfied.
fulfilling.example: Raising children can be very fulfilling.

rhythm.definition: A regular pattern of movement or sound.
rhythm.example: The conversation had a natural rhythm.

dominant.definition: Having more power or control than others.
dominant.example: One person should not be dominant in a conversation.

submissive.definition: Willing to obey others without question.
submissive.example: A submissive child may not express their true feelings.

exchange.definition: The act of giving and receiving between people.
exchange.example: A healthy exchange of ideas benefits everyone.

engage.definition: To attract and hold someone's attention.

pupils.example: The teacher asked her pupils to open their books.

tailor.definition: To change something to fit a particular need.
tailor.example: She tailored her teaching to suit the students.

brainstorm.definition: To think of many ideas quickly as a group.
brainstorm.example: Let's brainstorm ideas for the project.

peaceful.example: The classroom was a peaceful place.

whereas.definition: Used to compare two different things or situations.
whereas.example: She likes tea, whereas her sister prefers coffee.

resentful.definition: Feeling angry because you think something is unfair.
resentful.example: He became resentful after being ignored.

restless.definition: Unable to stay still or be patient.
restless.example: The children became restless during the long meeting.

frustrated.definition: Feeling upset because you cannot do or change something.
frustrated.example: She felt frustrated when no one listened to her.

unsatisfactory.definition: Not good enough; not meeting expectations.

heed.definition: To pay attention to or listen to advice.
heed.example: You should heed your doctor's advice.

hopeless.definition: Feeling that there is no hope; believing nothing will get better.

isolated.example: Living alone can make you feel isolated.

rebellious.definition: Refusing to obey rules or follow authority.

modelling.definition: Showing by example how to do something.
modelling.example: Parents are always modelling behaviour for their children.

steady.example: The baby needs a steady routine.

organization.definition: A group of people working together for a purpose.
organization.example: The organization helps children in need.

pairing.definition: Two things or people that are put together.
pairing.example: The parent-child pairing is the most important bond.
